section.title: David King Over All Israel
section.sectionRationale: The long wait ends: all the tribes gather at Hebron and anoint David king over all Israel, fulfilling the LORD's word that he would shepherd His people. David captures the Jebusite stronghold of Jerusalem and makes it his capital, grows greater because the LORD is with him, and twice inquires of the LORD before defeating the Philistines. The movement is the summit of David's rise, the throne finally given by God to His shepherd-king.
section.thesis: After years of waiting, God gives David the throne over all Israel, establishes him in Jerusalem, and grants him victory, so that David grows greater not by his own strength but because the LORD of Hosts is with him and has exalted his kingdom for the sake of His people.
section.frameNote: Four beats: all Israel anoints David king at Hebron, verses 1 to 5; the capture of Jerusalem and David's growing greatness, verses 6 to 12; David's expanding household, verses 13 to 16; and two victories over the Philistines won by inquiring of the LORD, verses 17 to 25.

verses.text: Then all the tribes of Israel came to David at Hebron and said, “Here we are, your own flesh and blood.

Even in times past, while Saul was king over us, you were the one who led Israel out and brought them back. And to you the LORD said, ‘You will {{raah|shepherd}} My people Israel, and you will be ruler over them.’”
So all the elders of Israel came to the king at Hebron, where King David made with them a {{berit|covenant}} before the LORD. And they anointed him king over Israel.
David was thirty years old when he became king, and he reigned forty years.
In Hebron he reigned over Judah seven years and six months, and in Jerusalem he reigned thirty-three years over all Israel and Judah.

units.label: David captures Jerusalem
units.note: the LORD of Hosts was with him

verses.text: Now the king and his men marched to Jerusalem against the Jebusites who inhabited the land. The Jebusites said to David: “You will never get in here. Even the blind and lame can repel you.” For they thought, “David cannot get in here.”
Nevertheless, David captured the fortress of Zion (that is, the City of David).

On that day he said, “Whoever attacks the Jebusites must use the water shaft to reach the lame and blind who are despised by David.” That is why it is said, “The blind and the lame will never enter the palace.”
So David took up residence in the fortress and called it the City of David. He built it up all the way around, from the supporting terraces inward.
And David became greater and greater, for the LORD God of Hosts was with him.
Now Hiram king of Tyre sent envoys to David, along with cedar logs, carpenters, and stonemasons, and they built a palace for David.
And David realized that the LORD had established him as king over Israel and had exalted his kingdom for the sake of His people Israel.

units.label: David's household grows
units.note: more sons and daughters in Jerusalem

verses.text: After he had arrived from Hebron, David took more concubines and wives from Jerusalem, and more sons and daughters were born to him.
These are the names of the children born to him in Jerusalem: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon,
Ibhar, Elishua, Nepheg, Japhia,
Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet.

units.label: Two victories over the Philistines
units.note: the LORD has gone out before you

verses.text: When the Philistines heard that David had been anointed king over Israel, they all went in search of him; but David learned of this and went down to the stronghold.
Now the Philistines had come and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim.
So David inquired of the LORD, “Should I go up against the Philistines? Will You deliver them into my hand?” “Go up,” replied the LORD, “for I will surely deliver the Philistines into your hand.”
So David went to Baal-perazim, where he defeated the Philistines and said, “Like a bursting flood, the LORD has burst out against my enemies before me.” So he called that place Baal-perazim.
There the Philistines abandoned their idols, and David and his men carried them away.
Once again the Philistines came up and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim.
So David inquired of the LORD, who answered, “Do not march straight up, but circle around behind them and attack them in front of the balsam trees.
As soon as you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the balsam trees, move quickly, because this will mean that the LORD has gone out before you to strike the camp of the Philistines.”
So David did as the LORD had commanded him, and he struck down the Philistines all the way from Gibeon to Gezer.

raah.gloss: To shepherd, to tend, feed, and lead a flock; used of a king caring for his people. The LORD's word is that David will shepherd Israel, drawing on David's literal work as a shepherd to define kingship as guardianship, not exploitation. The image runs forward to the LORD as Shepherd, Psalm 23, and to Christ, the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, John 10.

berit.gloss: Covenant, a solemn binding agreement sealed before God. David makes a berit with the elders of all Israel before the LORD, so that his kingship rests on a mutual, God-witnessed bond rather than mere conquest. The word reaches its height in the everlasting covenant God makes with David in chapter 7 and is fulfilled in the new covenant in Christ.

basic.context: This chapter is the summit of David's long rise. After seven and a half years reigning over Judah alone, and years of patient waiting since Samuel first anointed him, all the tribes of Israel now come to Hebron. They call David their own flesh and blood, recall that he had always led Israel in battle, and quote the LORD's word that David would shepherd His people. The elders make a covenant with him before the LORD and anoint him king over all Israel. David then captures the Jebusite fortress of Jerusalem, a city belonging to no tribe, and makes it his capital, the City of David. He grows greater and greater, the text says, because the LORD of Hosts is with him, and he comes to realize that God has established his kingdom for the sake of His people. When the Philistines attack to crush the new king, David does not presume; he inquires of the LORD before each battle and wins both by obeying God's specific directions. The chapter raises the questions of how David finally comes to the throne, why Jerusalem matters, and what the source of David's greatness really is.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative celebrating the establishment of David's kingdom, told to show that it was God's doing. It is not a promise that every faithful person will rise to prominence, nor does David's taking of more wives and concubines, verse 13, receive approval; the narrator reports it, and it will bear bitter fruit later, running against the law's warning that a king should not multiply wives. What the chapter presents for imitation is David's habit of inquiring of the LORD before acting and his recognition that his greatness came from God for the sake of God's people, not for his own glory.
basic.originalLanguages: The tribes ground their appeal in the LORD's word that David will raah, shepherd, His people, a word that defines the king as a shepherd who guards and feeds the flock rather than a lord who exploits it. David seals his kingship by making a berit, a covenant, with the elders before the LORD, so that his reign rests on a God-witnessed bond. Both words, shepherd and covenant, look forward to the Davidic covenant of chapter 7 and to Christ, the Good Shepherd of the new covenant.
basic.typology: David, born in Bethlehem and long anointed but only now enthroned over all Israel in Jerusalem, the shepherd-king whom the LORD makes great for the sake of His people, is a clear type of Christ. Jerusalem becomes the city of the great King, and David's throne there becomes the seat of a kingdom that Scripture will trace to the Messiah who reigns from the true Zion.
basic.christ: David is the shepherd-king given the throne by God, established in Jerusalem, growing great not by his own power but because the LORD is with him. This foreshadows Jesus, the Son of David and true Shepherd of His people, who is enthroned as King not for His own sake but for the sake of the people God gives Him. As David asked the LORD before every battle and won by God going out before him, so Christ did only the Father's will and won His victory as the Father worked through Him.
basic.god: God is the one who gives David the throne, fulfilling His long-standing word, and who is present with David so that he grows greater and greater. God establishes the kingdom not for David's glory but for the sake of His people Israel, showing that He raises up rulers to serve His purposes and care for His flock. And God is the warrior who goes out before David against the Philistines, so that the victories are His gift, not merely David's skill.
basic.crossReferences: First Samuel 16 records the anointing now fully realized. Psalm 78 celebrates God taking David from the sheepfolds to shepherd His people. Deuteronomy 17 warns kings not to multiply wives, a warning David's growing household begins to strain. And the New Testament presents Jesus as the Son of David and Good Shepherd who reigns for His people, in Matthew 2 and John 10.
basic.oneStory: The shepherd God chose long ago is finally king over all Israel, enthroned in Jerusalem, made great because the LORD is with him. This high point of the story, a covenant king reigning for the good of God's people from the city of the great King, points forward to Jesus, the Son of David and Good Shepherd, whose everlasting kingdom is established for the sake of all God's people.

context.literary: The chapter gathers a series of establishment scenes, coronation, capital, building, family, and victory, into a portrait of a kingdom firmly founded under God's blessing. The recurring refrain that the LORD was with David, verse 10, and had established him for His people's sake, verse 12, interprets every success as God's gift. The two Philistine battles, each opened by inquiring of the LORD, form a matched pair that closes the chapter on the note of dependence.
context.historical: Jerusalem lay on the border between Judah and the northern tribes and belonged to none of them, making it a shrewd, unifying choice for a capital. The Jebusite taunt about the blind and lame boasted of the fortress's strength; David took it by way of the water shaft. Hiram of Tyre's gifts of cedar and craftsmen reflect the international standing David's kingdom was gaining. The Valley of Rephaim southwest of Jerusalem was a natural invasion route the Philistines used.
context.canonical: This chapter completes the anointing of 1 Samuel 16 and establishes the Jerusalem monarchy that will dominate the rest of the Old Testament. Making Jerusalem the City of David and, soon, the home of the ark and the temple, sets the stage for Zion theology and for the Davidic covenant of chapter 7. The shepherd-king in Zion becomes a lasting image fulfilled in Christ, the Son of David who reigns from the heavenly Jerusalem.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that David's kingship over all Israel, his capital in Jerusalem, and his victories are the LORD's doing, granted in fulfillment of God's word and for the sake of His people, so that David's greatness is read as God's gift rather than David's achievement.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes David's coronation, conquests, and growing household without prescribing all of them; in particular, his taking of more wives and concubines is reported, not commended, and stands against the law's warning in Deuteronomy 17. What endures is David's dependence on the LORD, seen in his inquiring before battle, and the truth that God exalts rulers for the good of His people, not for their own glory.

debates.question: How should we read David's taking of more wives and concubines in verse 13?

views.label: A reported compromise, not an endorsement
views.case: The narrator simply records David's expanding harem without praise, and Deuteronomy 17:17 had warned that a king must not multiply wives lest his heart turn away; the very next chapters show David's household becoming a source of rivalry, lust, and violence, so the detail is best read as an early crack in an otherwise glorious chapter, honestly reported rather than approved.

views.label: A conventional mark of royal status in its setting
views.case: In the ancient Near East a large royal household signaled power and secured alliances, so some read verse 13 chiefly as a historical note on David's rising stature; even so, Scripture's larger judgment, and the bitter fruit in David's family, exposes it as description of a practice that God's law had cautioned against.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage is descriptive, not prescriptive; the glory of the chapter belongs to God's establishing of David, while the note about wives quietly foreshadows the troubles that David's own household will bring, reminding readers to weigh even a great king's actions against God's word.

study.originalLanguages: The tribes anchor their appeal in the LORD's own word that David will raah, shepherd, His people Israel, a term that casts the king not as an exploiter but as a shepherd who guards, feeds, and leads the flock, drawing directly on David's boyhood work with sheep. David then makes a berit, a covenant, with the elders before the LORD, so that his kingship rests on a mutual, God-witnessed bond rather than raw conquest. Twice more the chapter insists that David's rise is God's doing: the LORD of Hosts was with him, verse 10, and the LORD had established him and exalted his kingdom for the sake of His people, verse 12. Shepherd and covenant, the two governing words, point ahead to the everlasting covenant with David's house in chapter 7 and to Christ, the Good Shepherd of the new covenant.

typology.type: The shepherd anointed in obscurity, now enthroned as king in Jerusalem
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Son of David and Good Shepherd, enthroned as King over God's people, John 10:11 and Luke 1:32-33.

typology.type: Jerusalem, the City of David, seat of the kingdom
typology.fulfillment: The heavenly Jerusalem, city of the great King, from which Christ reigns, Hebrews 12:22 and Revelation 21:2.

typology.type: The king made great for the sake of God's people
typology.fulfillment: Christ exalted as King not for His own sake but for the salvation and good of His people, Ephesians 1:22-23.

study.christ: David is the shepherd-king given the throne by God, established in Jerusalem, and made greater and greater not by his own power but because the LORD of Hosts is with him. This foreshadows Jesus, the Son of David and true Shepherd of His people, John 10:11, enthroned as King not for His own glory but for the sake of the people the Father gives Him, Ephesians 1:22-23. As David inquired of the LORD before every battle and won because the LORD went out before him, so Christ did only the Father's will and won His decisive victory as the Father worked through Him. And the Jerusalem David founded becomes the shadow of the heavenly Zion from which Christ reigns forever, Hebrews 12:22.
study.god: God is the one who gives David the throne over all Israel, fulfilling the word He had long spoken, and who is present with David so that he grows greater and greater. God establishes the kingdom not for David's sake but, as David himself recognizes, for the sake of His people Israel, showing that He raises up rulers to serve His purposes and shepherd His flock. And God is the divine warrior who goes out before David against the Philistines, bursting out like a flood upon the enemy, so that the victories are His gift and the glory is His. The whole chapter reads David's greatness as the fruit of God's faithful presence.

crossReferences.note: Samuel's anointing of David, whose fulfillment reaches its climax as all Israel now anoints him king.
God chose David and took him from the sheepfolds to shepherd His people with integrity of heart, the shepherd-king image of this chapter.
The law's charge that Israel's king not multiply wives, against which David's growing household in verse 13 is measured.
Jesus, the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, the fulfillment of the shepherd-king promised to David.

study.oneStory: The shepherd God chose long ago, anointed in obscurity and made to wait through years of exile and civil war, is finally king over all Israel, enthroned in Jerusalem and made great because the LORD is with him. This summit of the story, a covenant king reigning from the city of the great King for the good of God's people, points forward to Jesus, the Son of David and Good Shepherd, whose everlasting kingdom is established for the sake of all God's people and who reigns from the true and heavenly Zion.

quiz.q: On what basis do all the tribes come to make David king?

options.text: They are his own flesh and blood, he had always led Israel in battle, and the LORD had said David would shepherd His people.
options.note: Verses 1-2 gather all three grounds, climaxing in the LORD's word that David would shepherd Israel.

options.text: David had conquered Jerusalem and proven himself the strongest available leader.
options.note: Jerusalem is taken after the coronation, and the tribes appeal to kinship and the LORD's word, not to conquest.

options.text: The LORD had promised that David would shepherd and rule His people.
options.note: True and central, but this option omits the kinship and David's proven leadership that the tribes also cite.

options.text: Samuel returned and commanded the tribes to crown David.
options.note: Samuel had died earlier; the tribes come of their own accord, appealing to the LORD's earlier word, not a fresh command from Samuel.

quiz.q: Why does the chapter say David grew greater and greater?

options.text: Because the LORD God of Hosts was with him, establishing his kingdom for the sake of His people.
options.note: Verses 10 and 12 credit David's greatness to the LORD's presence and purpose for His people.

options.text: Because David was a brilliant military strategist who never lost a battle.
options.note: David's skill is real, but the text attributes his greatness to the LORD being with him, not to strategy alone.

options.text: Because God was with him and had exalted his kingdom.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking purpose clause, that God exalted the kingdom for the sake of His people, not David's glory.

options.text: Because Hiram of Tyre made him wealthy with cedar and craftsmen.
options.note: Hiram's gifts are a sign of David's rising standing, not the cause of his greatness, which the text credits to the LORD.

quiz.q: What does David do before each battle with the Philistines?

options.text: He inquires of the LORD, and he wins by obeying God's specific directions, even circling behind them at the balsam trees.
options.note: Verses 19-24 show David inquiring and obeying, so the LORD goes out before him.

options.text: He relies on his experience and attacks the Philistines the same way both times.
options.note: David asks the LORD each time, and God gives different instructions for the second battle, which David obeys.

options.text: He asks the LORD whether and how to fight before engaging the enemy.
options.note: True, but this option omits the striking detail that David wins because the LORD Himself goes out before him to strike the enemy.

options.text: He consults his commanders and then trusts the LORD for the outcome.
options.note: The text stresses David inquiring of the LORD directly, not consulting his commanders, before each battle.

quiz.q: Why is David's choice of Jerusalem as his capital significant?

options.text: The Jebusite fortress belonged to no tribe, making it a unifying capital, and David made it the City of David, the seat of the kingdom.
options.note: Verses 6-9 show David taking neutral Jerusalem and establishing it as his royal city.

options.text: Jerusalem was already the religious center of Israel with the temple standing there.
options.note: There was no temple yet; David brings the ark later, and Solomon builds the temple, so the city's religious role is only beginning.

options.text: David captured a strong, central stronghold and made it his royal city.
options.note: True, but this option omits that its tribe-neutral location helped unite Israel and that it became the enduring City of David.

options.text: It was David's birthplace, which made it the natural royal city.
options.note: David was born in Bethlehem, not Jerusalem; he chose Jerusalem for its strategic and unifying value.

quiz.q: How does David as shepherd-king in Jerusalem point to Christ?

options.text: He foreshadows Jesus, the Son of David and Good Shepherd, enthroned as King for the sake of God's people and reigning from the true Zion.
options.note: David's shepherd-kingship in Jerusalem anticipates Christ, John 10:11 and Hebrews 12:22.

options.text: He shows that God's kings must always expand their kingdoms by conquest.
options.note: The chapter credits God, not conquest, and Christ's kingdom advances by His shepherding love, not mere expansion.

options.text: He pictures a king who reigns for the good of God's people rather than his own glory.
options.note: True, but this option omits the specific shepherd and Zion imagery that so clearly points to Christ the Good Shepherd.

options.text: He proves that greatness comes to those who seize their opportunities boldly.
options.note: The chapter teaches the opposite; David's greatness is God's gift because the LORD was with him, not the fruit of seizing opportunity.

questions: The tribes finally acknowledged what God had said about David years earlier. Where are you waiting for others to recognize something God has already spoken over your life, and how do you wait well?

David realized that God had established him not for his own sake but for the sake of His people. How does it reframe your position, gifts, or influence to see them as given for others rather than for yourself?
Before each battle David inquired of the LORD rather than trusting his experience. Where are you relying on your own track record instead of seeking God freshly?
The chapter credits David's greatness to the LORD being with him. When you succeed, how quickly do you name God as the source, and how might you cultivate that habit?

Even in this glorious chapter, David's growing household plants a seed of future trouble. Where might a choice that seems to signal blessing actually be a compromise you need to weigh against God's word?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote allows the phrase about the 'lame and blind' to be rendered those 'who are enemies of David'; the saying reflects the Jebusite taunt and became a proverb, and should not be read as contempt for the disabled as such.
The BSB footnote explains that Baal-perazim means 'the Lord Bursts Out,' commemorating David's words that the LORD had burst through his enemies like a flood.
The BSB footnote notes that the starting point of the pursuit reads 'Gibeon' following the Septuagint and 1 Chronicles 14:16, while the Hebrew reads 'Geba,' two nearby places.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 5:1-25. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced in this file.
